front: What is the primary difference between implementing the Runnable interface and extending the Thread class for creating a thread?
back: Implementing Runnable is preferred because Java does not support multiple inheritance. A class that extends Thread cannot extend any other class, whereas a class implementing Runnable can still extend another class. Runnable also separates the task from the thread object.

front: What is the purpose of the start() method in the Thread class, and why is it crucial to use it instead of directly calling run()?
back: The start() method tells the JVM to create a new thread of execution and then, asynchronously on that new thread, call the run() method. Calling run() directly executes the code in the current thread, defeating the purpose of multithreading.

front: How do you create and start two threads using the same Runnable instance?
back: You create a single instance of your Runnable class. Then, you pass that same instance to the constructor of two separate Thread objects. Calling start() on each Thread object will execute the same run() method code concurrently in two threads. Note: This requires careful synchronization if the threads modify shared data.

front: In the context of creating multiple threads, what is a common challenge, and what Java keyword is used to address it?
back: A common challenge is race conditions, where the outcome of a program depends on the unpredictable sequence of thread execution. The synchronized keyword is used to control access to critical sections of code, ensuring only one thread can execute that block at a time.

front: Write the code to create and start three threads, each printing "Hello from Thread " followed by its number.
